patch.value: Phase-1 message-surface rewrite: README hero, website narrative, GitHub About/topics, npm metadata, badge diet, terminology guide, generated counts

Rewrite every first-screen public surface around the ratified category and doctrine before any promotional push. Sending more visitors to contradictory positioning wastes attention — this phase precedes all distribution work in the program.

README opens with doctrine + category + under-60s real demo (no badge wall first); max 5 badges above the fold (npm, CI, license, Node, MCP/skills) with internal backlog badges moved to a dogfooding section; GitHub About + topics + npm description/keywords updated to the ratified copy; website hero leads with 'Give every coding agent the right project context' + three CTAs (install for my agent / watch demo / build with SDK); all public command/tool/capability/package counts generated from pm contracts + package catalog with labeled units; public terminology guide published (pm vs pm-cli usage rules); persona switcher or equivalent paths for agent users, maintainers, tool builders, package authors

# Phase-1 message-surface rewrite

## README (current problem: badge block above a generic descriptor, then doc routing, then a create/update example)
First screen must answer: what is this / why does an agent need it / what happens when I use it / can I try it now.

Recommended opening (post-ADR copy):

    # pm
    ### Your coding agent can write code. `pm` tells it what matters.
    `pm` is the repository-native project context engine for coding agents and software teams.
    It turns project work into compact, queryable, auditable context: the next valid task,
    durable plans, dependencies, decisions, evidence, and history — stored in Git and available
    through CLI, MCP, TypeScript SDK, and Web.
    **Project management is context management.**
    Local-first · Deterministic · Explainable · Replayable · Extensible
    [Install for an agent] · [CLI quickstart] · [Build with the SDK] · [See the demo]

Recommended README order: 1 hero+doctrine, 2 sub-60s demo, 3 choose-your-install (Agent/CLI/SDK), 4 why project context, 5 five-stage lifecycle (Capture->Rank->Coordinate->Prove->Remember), 6 works-with grid, 7 agent workflow, 8 human review workflow, 9 context quality + benchmark, 10 SDK quickstart, 11 packages+profiles, 12 architecture+trust model, 13 docs+community, 14 dogfooding+status, 15 license. Existing doc-routing table stays, but after the product is understood.

## The first demo (whole thesis in under a minute)
    npm install -g @unbrained/pm-cli
    pm init --defaults
    pm next            # recommended item + WHY + ready/blocked/ownership
    pm claim --next
    pm context --limit 10
Then evidence + closure:
    pm files <id> --add <path> --note "..."
    pm test <id> --add command="pnpm test -- auth" && pm test <id> --run
    pm close <id> "Acceptance criteria met; linked test passed." --validate-close strict
Finish with `git diff .agents/pm` — proves the repository-native claim visually.
Agent-handoff follow-up: `pm install github.com/unbraind/pm-brief --project && pm brief prompt --max-tokens 2000` — "a new agent receives ranked work, blockers, risks, activity, evidence, and safe next commands within a declared token budget."

## Website narrative
Section 1: "Agents do not need more context. They need the right context." with the dump-vs-pm table (hundreds of tasks vs the next valid work; repeated metadata vs budgeted projection; hidden blockers vs dependency-aware readiness; no explanation vs ranking reasons; session-only plan vs durable plan; done-assertion vs linked evidence; chat history vs replayable history). Section 2: five lifecycle cards, one outcome + one command each. Section 3: watch-an-agent-use-it (reuse the Claude plugin natural-language examples: what should I work on next / start the auth bug / close it). Section 4: works-with cards (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, VS Code/Copilot, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, other MCP clients, plain CLI) — label "native" ONLY when packaged and smoke-tested. Section 5: architecture diagram (agents -> pm MCP/skills -> context runtime -> .agents/pm in Git; human via CLI; tools/CI via SDK; pm Web on top). Section 6: proof (atomic/replayable mutation model, eval metrics, token-budget gate, contract snapshots, plugin smoke tests, package compatibility checks — the existing quality scripts ARE marketing proof). Section 7: benchmark section (see ContextBench item). Section 8: SDK+ecosystem led by outcomes, not export lists.

## Feature-count rule
"101 commands / 83 actions / 28 tools" as primary message reads as complicated, token-heavy, unstable. The outcome message is "the agent gets the right context and can act on it safely." Counts appear only as generated, labeled, secondary facts.

## SDK page (within this rewrite)
Headline: "Build project-aware developer tools without rebuilding project state." Three jobs up front: Embed pm (PmClient in bots/CI/portals) / Extend pm (commands, schema, profiles, importers, hooks, scorers) / Evaluate context (scenarios, budgets, thresholds, CI gates). Five recipes: read+rank context; safe agent lifecycle (search->create-or-reuse->claim->update->evidence->close); build a package (defineExtension -> lint -> synthesize manifest -> preflight -> harness -> doctor); define a project profile; evaluate a custom context scorer (the signature technical story). Never lead with hundreds of export names. Keep the SDK bundled (/sdk, /sdk/runtime, /sdk/testing) per the do-not-do fence on pm-wjfa.
